frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Suresh: Dekho, main blame nahi kar raha, but aajkal zamaana theek nahi hai. Ladkiyon ko bhi thoda practical rehna chahiye—late night bahar rehna, alag-alag logon ke saath ghoomna, phir kuch ho jaaye toh sawal toh uthte hi hain ki itni raat ko kyun thi aur saath mein kaun tha.
Wife: Haan, apni safety ka dhyan toh rakhna hi chahiye.
Suresh: Bas wahi, hum bhi agar kisi ko samjhayen ki thoda sambhal ke rahe, decent timing rakhe, toh galat kya hai? Duniya ko hum nahi badal sakte, apna bachav toh khud hi karna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames male violence as inevitable and shifts responsibility onto women to restrict their movement and behavior. It encodes protective framing, victim-questioning after harm, and the 'I'm just being practical' defense.
original_frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are discussing marriage proposals for your son Karan who is 27. A proposal has come from a good family Ñ the girl is educated, employed, from a respectable background. But you found out through a mutual contact that the girl has a large mixed gender social circle and is known to go out with male friends in groups. You are sitting with your wife telling her what you found out and what you think it means for the kind of wife she will be and whether this is the kind of girl you want for your son and what you are going to tell the girl's family.
